rationale: The citing paper discusses Nishikawa et al. (2017) multiple times in its core literature review, including a dedicated row in Table 1, and uses it as key evidence that user-designed/crowdsourced products can increase preference through quality inferences. It then positions its own contribution as explaining when such positive user-design effects do versus do not occur, introducing power distance belief and product complexity as moderators. This is more than a passing mention because the cited study helps anchor the theoretical problem the new paper seeks to resolve.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Nishikawa et al. (2017)'s positive user-design finding and qualifies it by showing boundary conditions. Specifically, it argues that preference for user-designed products is contingent on power distance belief (and product complexity), with high-PDB consumers often favoring designer-designed products via trust.
